fact: November 30 is exactly 30 days before the winter solstice in the Northern Hemisphere, making it like nature's countdown timer to the shortest day of the year!
In Antarctica, November 30 falls during their spring season when penguin chicks are getting their first real feathers and learning to waddle like pros.
This date marks the end of meteorological autumn in many places, which means winter officially starts tomorrow according to weather scientists (even though calendar winter waits until December 21).
November 30 is always exactly 334 days into the year, leaving just 31 days until New Year's Eve - you could count them all on your fingers and one toe!
In northern regions like Alaska and northern Canada, November 30 can have as little as 4 hours of daylight, making it feel like the sun is just taking a quick peek before hiding again.
Maple trees in temperate zones have completely dropped their leaves by November 30, creating crunchy carpets that make the most satisfying sounds when you stomp through them.
The moon phase on November 30 changes every year, but when it's a full moon on this date, it appears extra bright because the cold, clear late-autumn air acts like nature's magnifying glass.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, November 30 is peak spring - flowers are blooming everywhere and baby animals are being born left and right.
November 30 falls during deer rutting season in North America, when male deer get so focused on impressing females that they forget to eat and can lose up to 25% of their body weight.
The sun sets before 5 PM in most of the northern United States on November 30, making it one of those weird days when it feels like bedtime right after school ends.
In many cold climates, November 30 is when the first permanent snow cover of winter typically arrives and decides to stick around until spring - like nature's way of putting on its winter pajamas.
